theorem_title: Smoothing of four-valent polyhedral Lagrangian surfaces in R4
theorem_statement: Let \(L\subset \mathbb R^4\) be a polyhedral Lagrangian surface for the standard symplectic form, with exactly four two-dimensional faces meeting at every vertex. Then \(L\) admits a Lagrangian smoothing: there is a family of smooth Lagrangian submanifolds \(L_t\), \(t>0\), forming a Hamiltonian isotopy through smooth Lagrangians and converging by a topological isotopy to \(L\) as \(t\to 0\).
raw_proof_text:
The proof is local-to-global. We first put the singular model near each vertex into a standard symplectic form, then describe local smoothings as graphs of exact one-forms, and finally patch the local choices to obtain a global Hamiltonian smoothing.

Fix a vertex of \(L\). Near this vertex, the surface is the union of four linear Lagrangian quadrants. Let the four outgoing edge vectors be
\[
v_1,v_2,v_3,v_4,
\]
ordered cyclically, so that each adjacent pair spans one of the Lagrangian faces. Since each face is Lagrangian, we have
\[
\omega(v_i,v_{i+1})=0
\]
for adjacent indices. The non-adjacent pairings cannot both vanish unless all four vectors lie in a common Lagrangian plane, in which case the vertex would not be a genuine four-valent singularity. Therefore, after reordering and rescaling the vectors, we may arrange
\[
\omega(v_1,v_3)=1,
\qquad
\omega(v_2,v_4)=1,
\]
with the other pairings compatible with the standard symplectic basis. The ordered vectors \((v_1,v_3,v_2,v_4)\) then form a symplectic basis of \(\mathbb R^4\). Consequently, a linear symplectic change of coordinates sends the local vertex model to the product of two coordinate-crossing models in \(\mathbb R^2\). Thus every vertex has the same standard local form.

In this standard model, choose a Lagrangian plane \(P\) transverse to the vertical directions. Projection to \(P\) identifies the singular surface locally with a union of four quadrants in \(P\). Choosing a complementary Lagrangian plane \(P'\), each linear Lagrangian face is the graph of a linear map \(P\to P'\), equivalently the graph of the differential of a quadratic form on \(P\). Thus the four faces are described by a piecewise quadratic function \(q\) whose quadratic pieces agree to first order along their common edges.

A local smoothing is obtained by replacing this piecewise quadratic function by a smooth function with the same first-order asymptotics. More precisely, a function \(f\) on the local quadrant model is a smoothing function if \(f+q\) is smooth on \(P\). The graph of \(d(f+q)\) is then a smooth Lagrangian submanifold because graphs of exact one-forms in a cotangent model are Lagrangian. Conversely, any sufficiently small graphical Lagrangian smoothing over this local model is the graph of an exact one-form and hence arises from such a smoothing function, up to addition of a constant.

This description is independent of the auxiliary choice of complementary Lagrangian plane. Indeed, changing the complementary Lagrangian plane changes the quadratic reference function \(q\) by adding a global quadratic form. If \(f+q\) is smooth, then after the change the new expression is \(f+(q+Q)\), where \(Q\) is smooth everywhere. Thus smoothability is unchanged. Hence the local space of smoothing functions is intrinsic.

We next handle compatibility along edges. Near an edge, exactly two Lagrangian half-planes meet along a common line. The possible choices of transverse Lagrangian complements form a contractible space: after a linear symplectic change of coordinates, the problem reduces to choosing a line in a two-dimensional quotient that intersects a fixed positive quadrant in a controlled way, and that set of choices is convex, hence contractible. Therefore the local choices made near adjacent vertices can be connected consistently along each edge. Since the choices over edges are contractible and the choices over faces are also contractible, there is no obstruction to extending these local choices to a global family of transverse affine Lagrangian planes along the whole surface.

This global family is a dual conormal fibration: at each point \(x\in L\), it assigns an affine Lagrangian plane through \(x\), varying smoothly along strata and agreeing with the standard local models near vertices and edges. It gives a neighborhood of \(L\) the structure of a cotangent-like bundle over the singular surface, in which small exact one-form graphs are Lagrangian.

It remains to construct global smoothing functions. Around each vertex and edge, choose local smoothing functions of arbitrarily small \(C^1\)-norm. On the faces, take the zero smoothing function. Patch these using a partition of unity subordinate to small neighborhoods of the strata. The only point to check is that the derivatives introduced by the cutoff functions remain controlled. If the cutoff changes on scale \(\varepsilon\), its derivative is of size \(O(1/\varepsilon)\). But the difference between two local smoothing models vanishes to first order along the common stratum, so on the support of the cutoff derivative that difference is \(O(\varepsilon)\). Their product is therefore uniformly bounded, and by taking the neighborhoods small one obtains a global smoothing function with arbitrarily small \(C^1\)-norm.

Let \(f_t\) be a family of such global smoothing functions with \(\|f_t\|_{C^1}\to 0\) as \(t\to 0\). The graph of \(df_t\) with respect to the dual conormal fibration is a smooth embedded Lagrangian submanifold \(L_t\) for all sufficiently small \(t>0\). Since the \(C^1\)-norm tends to zero, these graphs converge topologically to the original polyhedral surface \(L\).

Finally, if \(t\) varies smoothly, the family of exact one-form graphs \(\operatorname{graph}(df_t)\) is a Hamiltonian isotopy: the time derivative of the primitive functions supplies the Hamiltonian generating the motion. Thus the smooth Lagrangians \(L_t\), for \(t>0\), form a Hamiltonian isotopy and extend continuously to the original polyhedral Lagrangian surface at \(t=0\). This proves the smoothing theorem.
